nativeRange.habitat: Tropical coral reefs, typically found on reef faces and overhangs where they establish cleaning stations among live rock and coral outcroppings; reported from the Red Sea throughout the Indo-West Pacific to the Coral Sea and southern Korea.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs for cleaning stations
stable, mature reef rockwork for shelter during molts
open ledges where fish can approach to be cleaned
tightly-fitting lid to reduce escape risk

diet.notes: Generally considered easy to feed. Performs natural cleaning behaviour on tankmates, picking parasites, dead tissue, and mucus from fish at established cleaning stations. Readily takes most prepared foods, including frozen mysis, brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, sinking pellets, and flake. Periodic supplementation of iodine and trace elements is commonly advised to support successful molting.

goodWith: tangs, wrasses, and other fish that visit cleaning stations
LPS, SPS, soft corals, and zoanthids (with adequate feeding)
avoidWith: large predatory crabs and mantis shrimp
any system recently treated with copper-based medications

summary: Iconic 5–6 cm Indo-Pacific cleaner shrimp with a central white stripe flanked by scarlet red bands. Sets up cleaning stations on live rock and removes parasites and dead tissue from fish, making it a foundational reef-tank invertebrate.
careNotes: Generally considered one of the most beginner-friendly marine shrimp and a near-default addition to community reef tanks. Establishes cleaning stations on prominent live rock and is regularly visited by tangs, wrasses, and other fish to have ectoparasites and dead tissue removed. Like all marine invertebrates it is highly intolerant of copper-based medications, and any history of copper treatment in the system should be regarded as disqualifying. Drip-acclimate slowly (an hour or more) — it is reported to be sensitive to sudden swings in salinity, pH, or temperature. Generally considered reef-safe; isolated reports exist of underfed individuals nipping at LPS or zoanthid polyps, so regular feeding (mysis, brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood) reduces the risk. Periodic iodine and trace-element supplementation is commonly advised to support molting. Source ranges varied modestly: temperature 22–28°C across sources (averaged to 23–27°C); specific gravity 1.023–1.026 across sources (Aqua Imports stated 35 ppt ≈ SG 1.026). Minimum tank size recommendations ranged from 10 to 20 US gallons; written here as 75 L (~20 gal) to give a pair adequate territory and avoid intraspecific aggression seen in cramped tanks.
breedingNotes: Hermaphroditic — any two individuals will pair. Specifically a protandric simultaneous hermaphrodite: juveniles mature first as functional males and later become simultaneous hermaphrodites capable of acting as either sex. Paired hermaphrodites alternate molting and role-reversal, with one brooding 200–500 greenish eggs attached to the pleopods. Eggs hatch into planktonic larvae that pass through many zoeal stages over roughly five to six months — captive rearing is widely regarded as extremely difficult, with very few larvae surviving to settlement in hobby setups.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied URL pointed to a stale catalog landing page; resolved to the current product page via search.

additional.name: Aqua Imports
additional.notes: Designated secondary. Product page is sparse ("description coming soon") but stated origin (Sri Lanka) and target parameters (pH 8.2–8.4, 74–78°F, 35 ppt salinity ≈ SG 1.026).

additional.name: Aquariumbreeder (Shrimp and Snail Breeder)
additional.notes: Detailed cross-check used to fill care gaps the trade pages omitted: temperature 25–28°C, SG 1.023–1.025, 20-gallon (~76 L) minimum, lifespan ~3 years, protandric simultaneous hermaphroditism, and difficult breeding.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Lysmata amboinensis)
additional.notes: Used only for taxonomy (family Lysmatidae, order Decapoda) and wild depth range (5–40 m). Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium Blog
additional.notes: Confirmed water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025), incompatibility with hawkfish/pufferfish/flame cardinalfish, and lifespan of three or more years in captivity.

additional.name: Aquariadise
additional.notes: Confirmed 20-gallon minimum tank size, severe copper sensitivity, and intraspecific aggression in cramped tanks (>1 pair not recommended below 20 gal).

crustacean.moltingNotes: Healthy adults generally molt roughly every three to six weeks; juveniles molt more frequently. Shed exoskeletons can be left in the tank as a calcium and chitin source. Stable salinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace-element levels (particularly iodine) support successful molts; sudden parameter swings are the most commonly reported cause of failed molts.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Protandric simultaneous hermaphrodites — any two individuals will pair and alternate male/female roles during mating. Brood 200–500 greenish eggs on the pleopods. Larvae are long-lived plankton (months in the wild) and have an extremely high mortality rate in captive rearing; rearing is widely considered impractical without a dedicated planktonic-larvae system.
